---
title: "Projects"
---

Project pages are where a research site becomes genuinely useful. They let you document work before it reaches the paper stage and give collaborators one stable link for context.

## What each project page should include

:::{.card-grid}
::: {.panel}
### Question

Describe the scientific problem in one short paragraph. If the problem statement is unclear, the rest of the page will feel scattered.
:::
::: {.panel}
### Approach

List the system, measurements, experiments, or analytical workflow that make the project possible.
:::
::: {.panel}
### Status

Note whether the work is exploratory, active, submitted, or archived. This saves everyone time.
:::
::: {.panel}
### Related outputs

Link directly to papers, code, talks, datasets, notebooks, or image galleries connected to the project.
:::
:::

## Suggested buckets

- Active projects
- Work in development
- Archived projects with reusable materials

::: {.note-box}
If a project becomes complicated, split it into one overview page plus smaller subpages for methods, figures, or field notes.
:::
